coredata.dc:title: Metal-doped carbon dots as robust nanomaterials for the monitoring and degradation of water pollutants

coredata.dc:description: The contamination of the environment by domestic and industrial discharges is a relevant and persistent problem that needs novel solutions. Innovations in the detection, adsorption, and removal or in-situ degradation of toxic components are urgently required. Various effective techniques and materials have been proposed to address this problem, in which carbon dots (CDs) stand out because of their unique properties and low-cost and abundant nature. Their combination with different metals results in the enhancement of their innate properties. Metal-doped CDs have shown excellent results and competitive advantages in recent times. Considering the above useful critiques and CDs notable potentialities, this review discusses different approaches in detail to sense, adsorb, and photodegrade different pollutants in water samples. It was found that altering the electronic structure of CDs via metal doping has a great potential to enhance the optical, electrical, chemical, and magnetic capabilities of CDs, which in turn is beneficial for wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The contamination of the environment by domestic and industrial discharges is a relevant and persistent problem that needs novel solutions. Innovations in the detection, adsorption, and removal or in-situ degradation of toxic components are urgently required. Various effective techniques and materials have been proposed to address this problem, in which carbon dots (CDs) stand out because of their unique properties and low-cost and abundant nature. Their combination with different metals results in the enhancement of their innate properties. Metal-doped CDs have shown excellent results and competitive advantages in recent times. Considering the above useful critiques and CDs notable potentialities, this review discusses different approaches in detail to sense, adsorb, and photodegrade different pollutants in water samples. It was found that altering the electronic structure of CDs via metal doping has a great potential to enhance the optical, electrical, chemical, and magnetic capabilities of CDs, which in turn is beneficial for wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water bodies have been mistreated, exploited, and polluted extensively due to population growth leading to the need to meet the global demand in industries such as food, textiles, and pharmaceutics. This phenomenon involves a series of problems related to the discharge of residues that end up contaminating the surrounding environment (Sudha et al., 2014), where the short- and long-term ecological and health consequences are usually disregarded by the economic progress.

Effluents are defined as untreated, partially treated, or entirely treated waste liquids produced and released into the environment by any industry or commercial institution, particularly acting as a substance that has a negative effect on the place of discharge; it may also be called “trade effluent” or “wastewater”. Unused inorganic and organic media components, microbial cells and suspended materials, filter aids, waste wash water from cleaning operations, cooling water, water containing traces of solvents, acids, alkalis, human sewage, and so on, are some examples of typical wastes (Han et al., 2018a). Classifying these pollutants is challenging due to a wide range of effluents produced by industrial plants, each with highly diverse activities and end products. Therefore, Thomas and Thomas (2022) found a method for examining the impact of the industry using the organic fraction of the effluent. They reported that waste from the food industry is frequently characterized by high concentrations of suspended solids as well as organic biodegradable pollutants from natural matrices. Chemical processes, such as refineries, preserve huge amounts of primary components and end products with little to no variation in the wastewater quality; thus, high organic pollution and difficulties for biodegradability are typically observed. The toughest industrial discharges to deteriorate are often those that relate to organic synthesis. Different types of minerals from steel producers exhibit low levels of organic contamination and a higher proportion of suspended particulates. Finally, miscellaneous types enter with major pollutants with medium to high salinity as consequences of selected chemistry and pulp and paper industry sectors.

Historically, garbage might be dumped directly onto a suitable piece of land or into a nearby stream (Han et al., 2018b). Since 2002, the 3rd World Water Forum in Kyoto, Japan, has brought awareness to the fact that every day, approximately 2 million tons of pollutants of various categories (industrial waste, agricultural waste, and sewage) are discharged into water around the world, an amount that is almost equal to the weight of the terrestrial population, producing nearly 1500 km3 of wastewater (Vasilachi et al., 2021). That is why these pollutants must be governed by a standard that establishes the minimum permissible values, as well as the most efficient disposal and treatment methods. Therefore, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) created the effluent guidelines, which is a regulatory standard with fixed norms for each industry depending on treatment and control technology performance. However, they are not always fulfilled, either due to budget restrictions for maintenance, repairs, or qualified personnel. Some examples of the direct problems that result from the interaction with flora, fauna, and humans are carcinogenic effects, infections, toxicity, the creation of pathogen resistance to antibiotics, endocrine system disruption, etc. (M. Kumar et al., 2020). Thus, novel strategies are required to efficiently treat contaminated water.

To date, several methods have been used to remove or remediate hazardous and organic pollutants. This review discusses the most recent developments in the creation of novel carbon-based materials. Here, we discuss contemporary compounds that have been crucial to the adsorption and photocatalytic process of hazardous water contaminants. This technology has the potential to be one of the most promising possibilities, as it satisfies the requirements of being efficient, inexpensive, quick, operationally practicable, and environmentally friendly to detect, monitorate, and degrade water pollutants (Xiang et al., 2019).

Carbon dots or fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are new competitive quasi-spherical-zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a carbon core consisting of crystalline sp2 carbon with rich amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on its surface. It can exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent behavior and excitation wavelength-independent fluorescent emissions (Li et al., 2022; Lim et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016). This trait has motivated an even deeper investigation in the CDs research in bioimaging, electro-optical and photonic materials, energy harvesting, environmental monitoring, and environmental remediation among others (Jelinek, 2017; Lin et al., 2018). CDs possess different advantages such as excellent photo-physical properties, facile synthesis procedures, low-cost fabrication, low toxicity, superb water solubility, and easy surface functionalization (Ghosal and Ghosh, 2019). Moreover, CDs can be obtained through environmentally friendly and low-costs methods if carbon-containing wastes are used as precursors; thus, converting a low-value waste material into a high-value nanomaterial (Fig. 1 ) (González-González et al., 2022a, 2022b).

Pristine CDs might present some limitations in their practical applications related to low values of quantum yield or ease of contamination. Thus, researchers have recently explored different strategies to enhance the CDs’ fluorescence properties and performances. In this context, surface passivation and chemical doping are two efficient approaches to increase the photoluminescence properties and broaden the application scope of CDs (González-González et al., 2022c). Doping is a useful method for controlling the physicochemical characteristics of CDs; it has attracted a lot of research interest in recent years due to its additional benefits in comparison to pristine CDs. The overlapping atomic orbitals of the heteroatoms and carbon atoms, as well as the push/pull electron action of heteroatoms, lead to modifications in the electronic structure, nanostructure, and chemical composition of heteroatom doped CDs. This increases the convenience for large-scale synthesis as it also confers excellent optical properties and different intrinsic physicochemical properties by introducing either non-metal atomic impurities or metal ions into CDs (Li et al., 2022). Doping CDs with metal ions can change the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs, causing observable changes in optical, electrical, and magnetic characteristics; metal ions have more electrons, empty orbitals, and a higher atomic radius than non-metallic atoms.

One of the most unique improvements is the catalytic performance; the introduction of metal ions is thought to have a significant impact on the spin density and charge distribution of CDs, increasing the density of catalytically active centers for binding redox species in catalytic processes (Lin et al., 2018). Metal doped CDs offer additional catalytic active centers, enhance catalytic activity, and boost binding selectivity in comparison to pristine CDs. Thus, researchers have reported widespread usage of metal-doped CDs in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, chemical catalysis, and biocatalysis (Li et al., 2022). Photocatalysis is an environmentally friendly and long-lasting process, with three critical features: wide absorption capacity, efficient electron transport, and suitable photoredox qualities (Zhang et al., 2018). During the photocatalytic process, the energy of the natural sunlight can be used and transformed into the electrical energy needed to produce free radicals; therefore, no extra energy is required for the process. As a result, methods using natural light have attracted a lot of interest as a green catalytic technique (Chu et al., 2019; Mu et al., 2019). In this manner, the main purpose of the current review is to examine the most recent developments in metal-doped CDs for various applications related to effluent contaminated water bodies whether for sensing, adsorption, or degradation. In addition, the potential challenges that this technology may have to face in a future large-scale application are discussed to suggest possible directions for further research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional entities of metal-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Among the large diversity of carbon-based materials and nanomaterials, CDs represent one of the most suitable alternatives for multiple applications due to their enhanced properties and outstanding features that have been observed through the years. Their advantageous features include low toxicity/cytotoxicity, ultra-small size, water-solubility, biocompatibility, abundance nature of precursors, and ease of functionalization, among others (Fig. 2 ) (González-González et al., 2022d; Lopez-Cantu et al., 2022). Nowadays, this material can be applied in multiple areas since it represents a viable solution for different current situations including bioimaging, cellular imaging, drug delivery, and others (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019).

To improve their performance in all applications, the optimization of some key characteristics is a recently pursued goal. Consequently, multiple strategies have been explored to regulate and improve the physicochemical properties, photophysical properties, and quantum yield of synthesized CDs. Researchers have demonstrated that the employment of doped CDs, mainly doped with metallic elements, is an effective approach to achieving such improvements; the natural characteristics of metal-doped CDs are significantly potentiated after adding of the metallic elements. Those modifications have notable relevance in different applications including the development of optical sensors, bioimaging, phototherapy, photocatalysis, and biocatalysis (Li et al., 2022).

It should be considered that non-metallic substances or materials called heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus have also been used as dopants, either individually or in combination. Due to the very similar atomic radio between nitrogen and carbon, nitrogen is the most used heteroatom element for the synthesis of doped CDs. They have exhibited excellent performances in various applications including the sensing of toxic elements in environmental samples (González-González et al., 2022c). Similarly, the removal of different contaminants such as dye pollutants has been widely explored as well, in which methylene blue is typically used as a model contaminant. As a representative example of nonmetal-doped CDs, Tadesse et al. (2018) reported the synthesis of Fe3O4@NCQDs NCs, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/magnetite nanocomposites. The synthesized material presented favorable results in terms of efficient adsorption capacities for dye pollutants (90.84%).

In this manner, nonmetal-doped CDs offer a feasible solution for environmental remediation, showing multiple advantageous features compared to pristine CDs. However, the dopant material is a critical factor in the obtained advantages (Tadesse et al., 2018); it has been reported that doping with metal ions –specifically with transition ions– could result in higher efficiencies since the charge density on the surface of the CDs can be altered. For that reason, the addition of metallic materials that improve the results or confer some other additional characteristics should be further investigated and implemented (Bandi et al., 2021). Doping heteroatoms have demonstrated significant effects on the photoluminescence behavior; however, the effect on the absorption capacity is rarely involved or studied. In the case of the metal-doped CDs, the absorption behavior is clearly presented or displayed; nevertheless, there is not enough information about the details of the optical effects. Moreover, when metallic elements are involved, some limitations can be observed in certain applications, for example in phototherapy due the toxicity issues (Guo et al., 2018).

The doping technique of single materials modifies the active sites of the surface, tuning the fluorescence properties. On the other hand, co-doping provides electronic properties through a synergistic interaction. Metals such as Zn, Cu, and Si when introduced as dopants into CDs also improve the quantum yield (Jana and Pal, 2017). As a result, these metallic doping agents could be considered a better alternative compared to the heteroatoms that are usually used in “Doping engineering” since they also present an increased ability to donate electrons, provide more unoccupied orbitals outside them, and present a larger atomic radius. In addition, one of the best improvements in the physicochemical properties of metal-doped CDs compared to heteroatom doping, is the charge transition forms between graphene matrix-metal ions and the enhancement of the ability to change the charge density and change the electronic distribution or structure (Tejwan et al., 2021).

According to Xiao et al. (2020), metal doping consists of the deposition of a fine distribution of metallic particles on the porous carbon surface and pore channels. Although the general integrity of the material is maintained, some certain alterations occur, mainly improving the adsorption capacity. The doping techniques involve the gradual addition of different metals as “doping agents” including Zn, Mg, Ag, Au, Cu, Ga, Ni, Mo, and Gd. Each of them confers the ability to enhance some of the functional entities and properties of the pristine CDs depending on the metal that is used as an agent. Thereby, this technique represents an improvement in the characteristics of the material.

The preparation of metal-doped CDs can be achieved through different synthesis methods including solvothermal, hydrothermal, reduction, and microwave irradiation methods, among others (Fig. 3 ). Since it is a relatively new alternative, it should be noted that there is a lack of information about the doping principle itself (Li et al., 2022). The hydrothermal method is one of the most used techniques and consists of the interaction between two solids under specific conditions of temperature and pressure using an aqueous solvent. Similar to the hydrothermal method, the solvothermal technique requires comparable specific conditions and methodologies; however, a non-aqueous solvent is used, and a higher temperature is required. On the other hand, the sol-gel method consists of multiple procedures beginning with the synthesis of catalyst monomer or oxide gel, to the formation of a Metal-O-Metal bond that passes to a cross-linked gel. In this process, the pH and the temperature are fundamental factors, as well as other parameters such as solution composition, saturation, precipitation agents, and additives (V. Kumar et al., 2020).

Other important functional entities of the metal-doped CDs that represent additional advantages are the high optical absorbance and the enhancement of the quantum yield, antimicrobial activity, electrical conductivity, and photoluminescence property due to the increased electron-donating and accepting abilities and to the large number of organic functional groups. These modifications lead to a better catalytic performance in comparison to pristine CDs or other functionalized CDs, but still needs more research in order to discuss the key challenges (Tejwan et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for the sensing and monitoring of pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of pollutants by colorimetric or fluorescent sensors is considered a technique with great potential. This is due to its facility to illustrate the presence or high concentration of one or more compounds of interest, along with its great stability and multiple binding sites. The most common sensing approaches encompass photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), charge transfer, and the inner filter effect (IFE) (Li et al., 2022).

A novel Fe2+-doped CDs was synthesized by simple aqueous heating at 80 °C, to improve the fluorescence characteristics of the CDs and increase the detection specificity. Cysteine was coupled with Fe2+-doped CDs as the reference signal, and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), as functioning sensors to create a dual-emissive nanohybrid for the detection of Zn2+ and histidine. The fluorescence intensity of Au NCs is increased in the presence of Zn2+, resulting in the nanohybrid's emission turning pink. While the detection of histidine was shown by fluorescence color changes from pink to blue to pink. This approach demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity, allowing for more sensitive visual detection in real water samples (Han et al., 2018a).

Zn-CDs have demonstrated good selectivity for Hg2+, which is a toxic element that is prone to bioaccumulation thus negatively impacting plant, animal, and human health. Because of its propensity to enhance electron/hole recombination annihilation, Hg2+ induced the efficient quenching of Zn-CDs via an alternative and efficient electron transfer mechanism. It was shown that as the concentrations of Hg2+ rose, the fluorescence intensity of the Zn-CDs decayed. The Zn-CDs proved to have high specificity for Hg2+ sensing (Xu et al., 2018).

With the same basis of photoluminescence quenching, novel Cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots (C-CDs) have been synthesized and successfully employed to detect Cr(VI) ions. Because of its widespread use in stainless steel, vehicle components, and leather goods, chromium has received attention as a dangerous pollutant. Recently, South Africa experienced serious surface water pollution and associated drinking water contamination by Cr(VI) surrounding smelters. The interaction between Cr(VI) and the surface groups of C-CDs causes the IFE, which severely decreases the excitation of surface-related emission and, as a result, reduces the whole emission band via the FRET effect. The authors demonstrated how the color and fluorescence of C-CDs solution changed after adding Cr(VI) under natural and UV light, proving that C-CDs have the potential for recognizing Cr(VI) in real-life samples (Zhang et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for pesticide detection
ce:section.ce:para: Chromium(III)-doped carbon dots (Cr-CDs) were utilized to develop a fluorometric test for the detection of p-nitrophenol (p-NF), which is an extremely hazardous pesticide and a primary urine metabolite of parathion. This Cr-CDs-based nanoprobe exhibited a broad linear range (0.8150 μM), low detection limits (0.27 μM), and excellent sensitivity to p-NF in its detection in water. Since the Cr(III) dopant reacts with electrons on the surface of the Cr-CDs as well as the large aromatic molecule of the graphene-analogous Cr-CDs, the Cr-CDs present excitation-independent fluorescent characteristics (blue-green) and a high fluorescence quantum yield, as well as graphene-analogous structure and minimal cytotoxicity (Li et al., 2019). The synthesis method followed to prepare Cr-CDs is depicted in Fig. 4 .

Thiophanate-methyl (TM) is a benzimidazole fungicide commonly used in agriculture, long-term exposure poses a considerable danger to human health because of its teratogenicity, toxicity, and carcinogenic potential. A ratiometric fluorescent sensor with a synchronous response of dual emission was developed with Cu-CDs. The sensor identifies TM via a π−π stacking interaction between the aromatics of TM and also the possible chelation of Cu2+ of Cu-CDs through metal ion coordination, resulting in a shift in Cu2+ fluorescence emission due to twisted intramolecular charge transfer. As a result, the drawbacks of the ratiometric fluorescent sensors' single response are overcome, providing the Cu-CDs sensor with high sensitivity and anti-interference toward TM, ensuring high accuracy in treating TM containing residues (Han et al., 2019).

Co-doping is also an emerging approach to further improve the properties of CDs, such as in the case of the development of Mg,N-CDs to detect organophosphorus compounds such as paraoxon, which is a commonly utilized insecticide that if released become a significant risk to human health and the ecosystem. The analytical technique is based on employing pralidoxime (PAM) as a linker between the donor and the quencher, so the paraoxon concentration is calculated by measuring the quenching effect of the Mg,N-CD-PAM system. The results showed Mg,N-CDs with a high quantum yield of 33%, a rapid fluorescent response, a restricted size range, good water dispersibility, great sensitivity, and a low detection limit, making them a suitable tool in the fields of environmental monitoring and food safety (Peng et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for pharmaceutical compounds detection
ce:section.ce:para: Isoniazid (INH) is a drug that in excessive amounts in the human body can induce serious problems; therefore, sensors capable of detecting extremely small levels of this drug need to be designed. A Cu-doped Tragacanth/Chitosan carbon dot (Cu-GT/CS/CD) was synthesized and utilized to detect INH via a fluorescence OFF-ON mechanism based on a redox reaction between INH and Fe3+ as a quencher. The test showed that Cu-GT/CS/CD has good optical characteristics and photo-thermal stability, as well as a high potential for detecting very low levels of INH in wastewater samples (Shekarbeygi et al., 2019).

The removal of antibiotics from the environment represents a significant challenge because the more time they remain there, they can cause the growth of drug-resistant bacteria. Given this, Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe–N@CDs) and iron oxide-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles have been developed for the detection and degradation of doxycycline. The Fe–N@CDs selectively detect doxycycline with a limit of detection value of 66 ng mL−1 and a linear range of 0 mg mL−1 to 50 mg mL−1, deeming them a quick, sensitive, and cost-effective technology capable of ensuring food and environmental safety (Kaur et al., 2022).

Histamine (His) is the cause of scombroid fish poisoning, and it can induce a variety of toxicological symptoms; unfortunately, it is still difficult to quickly identify histamine in aquatic products due to complex matrixes and a lack of appropriate reagents. The existing methods to detect His require time-consuming sample extraction and purification, as well as complex derivation processes. In order to find a new approach, Fe and Co co-doped CDs (Fe, Co-CDs) with high peroxidase-like activity were produced in a straightforward hydrothermal manner and tagged with an anti-His mouse monoclonal antibody (Fig. 5 ). Histidine concentration in fish samples was rapidly determined with great sensitivity and accuracy using the ic-ELISA method, which used Fe, Co-CDs to competitively react with solid-phase antigens and sample antigens. This demonstrates that CDs can also be used in conjunction with existing methods to improve the detection of contaminants (Li et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for the detection of other chemical compounds
ce:section.ce:para: Fang et al. (2019) reported the detection of p-NP by the employment of copper-doped CDs. The p-NP is a compound used for the development of multiple chemicals as a raw material for pesticides, medicines, dyes, and rubber additives, among others. It represents a latent threat to health and can also provoke severe harm to the environment. For that reason, it is enlisted as a priority pollutant control by the EPA. This compound can be absorbed by the skin when it comes to short-medium term exposures. For long-term exposure most worrying and serious consequences can be presented since it can stimulate and inhibit the central nervous system, provoking multiple diseases. Consequently, it is vital to develop novel technologies to detect and remove them from different samples to avoid such consequences (Fang et al., 2019).

In order to obtain the Cu-CDs a simple hydrothermal technique is used. First, a precursor is required, using 1 mL of ethanediamine and 0.1 g of CuCl2·2H2O. Using a water bath sonication these two compounds were dissolved as a pretreatment. Then, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 10 h to obtain a solution that was centrifuged for further purification through dialysis. As a result, a green synthesis approach was followed to synthesize Cu-CDs with high selectivity and sensitivity for the target contaminant, presenting a fluorescence quantum yield of 7.8%. The nanomaterial was applied in water samples, demonstrating good applicability for environmental monitoring (Fang et al., 2019).

Nitrogen and copper-doped CDs have been developed aimed to detect pollutants like phenols. These doped CDs present a peroxidase-mimicking enzyme useful for the detection of contaminants. A facile hydrothermal carbonization method was followed to obtain the final product. As a result, the determination of phenols in sewage was successfully achieved with a low limit of detection and satisfactory recovery (Du et al., 2021). In addition, for the detection and fluorometric determination of metal ions such as iron (Fe3+) and copper (Cu2+), the Ni-doped CDs have been presented as a viable alternative according to Sun et al. (2022). Fe3+ and Cu2+ metal ions represent a health concern since even at trace levels could cause several damages. Copper and iron pollution in water and soil needs to be solved and avoided since it represents a health threat. In this context, metal-doped CDs were synthesized by an electrolysis method using a mixture of Ni–N–C and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) coating, followed by additional procedures to obtain the final product with the desired characteristics. As a result, the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ was achieved; the quantitation range of concentrations of Fe3+ was around 100–1000 μM and for Cu2+ was 300–900 μM (Sun et al., 2022). These studies demonstrate the feasibility to synthesize CDs through simple methodologies for the detection of multiple metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for pollutant adsorption and removal
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most pursued applications of these metal-doped CDs is the adsorption and degradation (removal) of different pollutants, mainly when they are contaminating water bodies. Water is a vital resource in multiple aspects of life since it is required in daily activities and for many production activities. Therefore, it is relevant to research and develop innovative alternatives to solving the current environmental issues associated with water pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for metal removal
ce:section.ce:para: Yang et al. (2021) reported a Metal-Ion-Cross-Linked Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots (NCDs) Hydrogel that could be used for both detection and removal of multiple heavy metals ions, principally divalent such as Pb, Cu, Ni, Co, and Cd from water. NCDs were prepared by a low-temperature sintering process, in which multiple surface groups were attached to their surface, creating the negatively charged surface. Interestingly, this electronegativity was used to easily combine with the positively charged metal ions to form a reversible hydrogel through charge attraction and coordination. As a result, a removal rate of almost 95% was obtained for Pb2+, proving to be an easy and fast alternative for heavy metal ions removal. In addition, the synthesized CDs-based material presented excellent results in sensitively detecting metal ions, thus showing great potential for both necessities: ultrasensitive detection and effective removal of metal ions in contaminated water.

Hui et al. (2021) reported the removal of copper (II) using N-doped and Bi-doped CDs. Bismuth was selected as the dopant agent due to its inherent characteristics such as non-toxicity and low thermal conductivity. Moreover, the electrical, optical, and chemical properties of materials can be enhanced by the deposition of bismuth onto the materials’ surface. N- and Bi-doped CDs were able to remove Cu (II) to different extents, around 56% and 33%, respectively. The authors concluded that the adsorption of the targeted metal onto doped CDs followed a pseudo-second kinetic model and Langmuir isotherm, showing adsorption capacities of 5.73 mg/g and 4.08 mg/g, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for dyes removal
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays the adsorption of dyes using different materials –including, metal-doped CDs– has been of great interest since dyes are one of the most common pollutants with a large annual production exceeding 700,000 tons. Consequently, they represent a latent danger to human and animal health and a potential threat to the environment due to their complex structures and the fact that most of them are non-biodegradable, toxic, and carcinogenic (Xiao et al., 2021). Multiple solutions have been proposed and evaluated such as filtration, irradiation, and others; but adsorption has been the one that shows the best results since it is more efficient, simple, and economically viable (low cost). In this regard, porous carbon materials have shown interesting performances, leading to the recent exploration of metal-doped CDs (Xiao et al., 2021). For instance, Hui et al. (2021) prepared CDs using rice husk as a biowaste precursor. CDs were subsequently doped by two different approaches: (1) using ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source to create N-doped CDs; and (2) using bismuth nitrate pentahydrate as a bismuth source to create Bi-doped CDs. Both CDs were evaluated in terms of methylene blue (MB) degradation; under optimal conditions, N-doped CDs could degrade 72% of MB, while Bi-doped CDs degraded 68% of the same dye.

In this manner, carbon-based quantum dots techniques have exhibited excellent results. Carbon-based quantum dots were developed as an evolution of the semiconductor quantum dots to overcome their typical drawbacks such as cytotoxicity and biohazard while maintaining many of their unique properties. As an example, Rajabi et al. (2018) reported the synthesis and development of pure doped zinc sulfide quantum dots for their application of organic dye removal from aqueous media. Nowadays, multiple types of dyes are contaminating the environment. In the study, the authors selected Victoria blue R (VBR) as the target contaminant since it can generate negative environmental impacts and could be toxic to non-target organisms. For the synthesis of the CDs-based material, a sono-assisted chemical precipitation method was followed using 250 mL of zinc nitrate solution at room temperature. Then a capping agent solution was added dropwise to the zinc solution followed by sodium sulfide using continuous ultrasonic irradiation. To separate the desirable product, centrifugation was performed to finally perform a washing process with distilled water and a drying process for 18 h at 50 °C. As a result, a 95% removal of the VBR dye was obtained at the optimal conditions, demonstrating an excellent photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for pollutant photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: Different ways of improving the photodegradation of contaminants are developed constantly as solutions for the in-situ remediation of effluent contaminated waters. Metal ion doping gives CDs special catalytic capabilities and excellent performance by lowering the surface function of CDs, modifying their bandgap, and promoting electron transport between the processes. Recently, numerous studies have been conducted on the application of metal-doped CDs for photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for dye photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis using CDs has been applied for the degradation of different dyes. For example, Mu et al. (2019) created Cu,N-CDs by pyrolyzing Na2[Cu (EDTA)] and ascorbic acid at 250 °C for 2 h. Then, it was combined with Ag3PO4 to create a nanomaterial called “Cu,N-CDs/Ag3PO4” that was used to study the degradation of neutral red (NR) in the presence of visible light. The Cu,N-CDs' capacity to capture electrons was improved, leading to better surface electron transport, since the N in these materials has a stronger affinity for electrons with Cu than that of the C. In addition, the Ag3PO4 valence band's positively charged holes (h+) as it reacted with H2O to create free OH radicals, which took part in the breakdown of NR. The degradation efficiency of 95.5% within 60 min was achieved under LED irradiation.

In another work, Song et al. (2019) prepared co-doped CDs using both N and P. Then, CDs-ZnO composites termed “NPCQD/ZnO″ were synthesized using multi-shelled ZnO microspheres through a facile electrostatic absorption process. This novel study considered new factors as there was little information on the CDs with multi-shelled structures or multi element doping. After characterization, the results revealed that carbon quantum dots were uniformly dispersed across the inner and outer shells of ZnO. For the study of photocatalytic activity, the authors used simulated wastewater contaminated with MB. The results indicated that the degradation constant reaction for NPCQD/ZnO is two times greater than that of pure ZnO. Moreover, the decoration of ZnO with CDs successfully reduced the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons–holes in the photocatalysts, thus, the composite showed lower fluorescence intensity. CDs had a main role in the enhanced photocatalytic performance. For instance, ZnO composites presented a higher degree of defects and surface oxygen vacancies due to the introduction of CDs into the system, thus improving the visible light response of the hybrid photocatalyst. On the other hand, dopant agents added P-containing, N-containing, and O-containing surface groups in CDs, which were associated with the formation of a miniature electric field in the material, promoting a better transfer of photogenerated electrons-holes. In conclusion, it was achieved a controllable preparation of CDs-decorated multi-shelled ZnO photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic properties and good stability even after 5 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-doped carbon dots for antibiotic photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic frequently released in effluents derived from pharmaceutical industries. The direct release of TC-containing effluents into the aquatic environment possesses a threat to human health and the ecosystem balance. In order to solve this problem in a green, low-cost, and safe manner, CDs-based systems have been proposed. For instance, a CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite was constructed by a simple impregnation-thermal method. Briefly, the g-C3N4 was synthesized by annealing melamine in a muffle. The ZnO nanorods were prepared from zinc acetate sintered at 580 °C for 2 h in an air atmosphere and then grounded into a white powder that was combined by chemical deposition with g-C3N4 in different proportions (10 wt%, 30 wt%, and 50 wt%). Subsequently, CDs were synthesized by an electrochemical method and dispersed in ultrapure water to form a CDs solution of 500 mg/L. This solution was mixed with the g-C3N4/ZnO and vigorously stirred for 2 h, the solvent was vaporized in an oven at 100 °C. Finally, the powder was annealed for 1 h at 300 °C in air, forming the CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposites with different loads of CDs (2 wt%, 4 wt%, and 6 wt%). The results demonstrated that even under incredibly low visible light irradiation (>420 nm, 18 mW/cm2), the CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO with 4 wt% displayed exceptional photocatalytic activity for complete TC degradation. It was able to degrade 100% of the target after 30 min of reaction. CDs played a fundamental role in the obtention of such excellent photocatalytic results since not only were able to respond towards a wider spectrum of visible light but also inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes due to its great electron transfer (Guo et al., 2017).

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), as a fluoroquinolone of the second generation, is used to treat bacterial infections in both humans and animals. Large quantities of its active substance are constantly discharged, caused by the huge amounts of production and consumption of this pharmaceutical compound. Thus, researchers have studied effective methods for the degradation of this antibiotic. As a representative example, Mou et al. (2019) presented a simple hydrothermal process to manufacture ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) BiOCl/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (BiOCl/NGQDs) composites that enhance the adsorption and photodegradation of CIP. The hybrid photocatalyst presented improved performances in comparison to the ones exhibited by pure BiOCl. The photocatalytic degradation tests were performed in a top-irradiation quartz vessel, kept at room temperature by a circulating water system. Firstly, the adsorption equilibrium between the photocatalysts and CIP pollutants was achieved by adding 50 mg of sample to 100 mL of CIP aqueous solution and stirring it in the dark for 30 min before irradiation. During the specified period, a sample of the suspension equal to 3 mL was taken and centrifuged. Then, the distinctive absorption band of 272 nm was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer where the residual concentrations of CIP were determined. The degradation efficiency of 82.5% was achieved after 60 min of exposure to visible light. This was a significant improvement compared to the pure BiOCl that degraded 34.9% of the sample. Regarding the total organic carbon, a removal efficiency of 95.5% after 5 h was achieved; a major portion of CIP was successfully mineralized into CO2, H2O, and other inorganic compounds. In addition, BiOCl/NGQDs showed higher values of surface area calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method, thus suggesting a greater adsorption ability for CIP. Graphene quantum dots had a relevant function to obtain the enhanced photocatalytic activity; improved visible-light response, effective inhibition of the recombination of electrons-holes, and excellent adsorption capacities were observed (Mou et al., 2019).

The presence of different pesticides represents serious environmental issues as well. Thus, photocatalysis assisted by CDs has been studied to achieve complete degradation of various pesticides. For instance, glyphosate as a common herbicide has been used as a target to test the photodegradation efficiency of a CDs-based photocatalytic system. Phophayu et al. (2019) prepared graphene quantum dots-ZnO nanocomposites by adding 1 mL of graphene quantum dots solution into zinc nitrate, which was then mixed with NaHCO3 at a flow rate of approximately 2 mL/min under continuous stirring and heating. After 1 h, the mixture was filtered and heated at 70 °C for 5 h. Finally, a calcination process was performed at 550 °C for 6 h to obtain the final photocatalyst. The degradation efficiency was calculated by the absorbance of a glyphosate solution under direct sunlight where the highest peak of degradation appears at 435 nm. The degradation efficiencies were found to be 12.07% for pure ZnO and 14.05% for GQDs-ZnO nanocomposites, after 180 min of photocatalytic process. Moreover, greater degradation constants by the synthesized nanocomposite (
k r = 8.64 ×
10
− 4
min
− 1
) was also demonstrated in comparison to that of pure ZnO (
k r = 7.02 ×
10
− 4
min
− 1
). For the synthesized nanocomposite, the kr was considerably enhanced by up to 23%. Because of their low cost and eco-friendly attributes, this work shows that graphene quantum dots-ZnO nanocomposites can be employed as a photocatalytic material for decomposing commercial herbicide pollutants, which might be useful for agricultural wastewater treatment (Phophayu et al., 2019).

In recent years, metal-doped CDs have raised interest for their numerous advantages for the ultrasensitive detection and degradation of an ample range of pollutants. This is achieved thanks to the great diversity of low-cost carbon sources, straightforward and cost-effective synthesis, practical surface functionalization, good water solubility, stability, and tunability (Zhang and Yu, 2016). The development and applicability of CDs are still in their early stages; therefore, several challenges remain unresolved. For instance, the complete mechanism and fundamentals of the doping processes are not yet entirely comprehended, thereby, obstructing precise control over the process and further optimization.

Usually, structural characterization techniques fail to establish whether the metal ions are in the lattice of the CDs or connected to their surface. Therefore, further investigation on the physical and chemical nature of metal-doped CDs should be conducted (Li et al., 2022). The incorporation of metal ions with a magnetic nature (e.g., Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, etc.) into CDs nanostructures has greatly improved the physicochemical and magnetic properties of CDs. CDs with magnetic properties are potential candidates for the separation of a wide range of chemical substances with simple sample preparation, providing remarkable advantages such as selectivity, sensitivity, rapidity, and effective extraction. Also, metal-doped CDs have demonstrated good performances for the extraction and identification of a wide variety of chemical species from complex samples, as well as an efficient sensor for the selective identification of ultra-trace target analytes with a lower limit of detection (Kailasa and Koduru, 2022). The recovery of the CDs after their usage is essential to achieve an environmentally friendly detection/removal of pollutants; therefore, taking advantage of the magnetic properties of metals can facilitate their recovery, enabling recycling with high yields.

Large-scale applicability can be optimized; most studies on the synthesis of CDs only describe the production of CDs under small-scale conditions –typically at the gram scale– (Zhang and Yu, 2016) and present irregularities in the preparation procedures such as insufficient purification. Novel metal-doped CDs-based sensors look towards the goal of good stability, selectivity, and broad limits of detection of various types of pollutants. While the researchers studying metal-doped CDs applied for degradation purposes search for enhanced removal efficiencies with facile recovery, good reusability, and greater target selectivity mainly obtained by the addition of functional groups. Overcoming the current limitations may pave the way for metal-doped CDs-based bioremediation and the exploration of different applications.

In this review, it was demonstrated that carbon dots processing has a great potential to tackle different problems that society faces nowadays. CDs are low-cost, unique, and adaptable nanomaterials that, in combination with different metals, have proved to be a novel approach with great potential to enhance their innate properties. It is evident the great advancements achieved by the scientific community regarding CDs' applications for sensing, adsorption, and degradation of various pollutants. However, further research regarding the optimization of CDs’ key features is still needed. Moreover, there are limited studies regarding the applicability of metal-doped CDs, in comparison to CDs doped with other nonmetal agents. Thus, additional research efforts should be made in this direction. Studies comparing nonmetal and metal-doped CDs in terms of characterization and performances are also required. Modifications of the optical, electrical, chemical, and magnetic capacities of pristine CDs by metal doping might become a suitable alternative for wastewater treatment in the near future.
